title: Introduction to Arabic Pronouns and Question-Response Structures
summary: This session focuses on using pronouns in questions and responses, emphasizing correct case endings (rasm) and gender agreement. Students practice forming sentences with ka (you), hu (he), ha (she), and ya for addressee. Key vocabulary includes family terms like abun (father), umun (mother), akhun (brother), and xalilatun (friend).
key_points: Pronouns in questions require correct case endings: ka (for you) with fathah, hu (he) with damma, ha (she) with kasrah.
Responses use the same pronoun from the question. E.g., 'Aina abooka?' → 'Abuuka fi sayyarati.'
Family terms follow gender agreement: abun (father), umun (mother), akhun (brother), xalilatun (female friend).
Using possessive pronouns with lam (li) for humans vs. Andersen objects: 'Aindaka qalamun?' → 'Ma'aandi qalamun.'
Vocabulary practice includes common nouns like sayyaru (car), daftaru (notebook), and tajiru (merchant).
questions: [00:14:00] Q: How to translate 'Where is Hamsa from?' — A: Use 'Hamsatu min ayna? Min Juddah.'
[00:23:00] Q: Difference between Mubaridun (male nurse) and Mubaridatun (female nurse) — A: Gender endings determine meaning.
